Old bones stir where weeds grow through cracked flagstones. The skeletons rise with rusted blades as if answering a command no one can hear.
One broken statue watches the courtyard from a parapet with too much patience. It moves only when the party passes beneath it.
Roots have split the hall floor and filled the air with wet green rot. The vines drag themselves free when disturbed.
A cracked arch still bears the weight of a collapsed upper room. Finding the stress lines reveals how to pass without bringing it down.
A loose hearthstone hides a small cache wrapped in blackened cloth. Whoever hid it never came back after the fire.
Dusty boards give a soft warning creak before breaking over a cellar stair. Falling through means landing among old nails and broken crockery.
A fresh name has been scratched into dust on an ancient wall. The letters point toward a sealed room that should not have been opened.
A surveyor's chalk marks lead into the ruins and stop at a doorway filled with cold air. Their notes lie scattered nearby.
A bronze bell lies half buried where no tower remains. At sunset it rings once, and something beneath the stones answers.
Nettles crowd a sunny courtyard where old walls keep the wind away. Gloves and care turn the sting into useful greens.
Mushrooms grow along a damp cellar wall where rain drips through cracked stone. Several poisonous lookalikes grow in the same patch.
Old apothecary beds have gone wild between paving stones. Useful herbs still grow from the sheltered cracks.
A roofed cistern holds pale fish that have lived generations in the dark. They scatter at the first splash of falling dust.
A cracked fountain still holds green water and stubborn carp. Clearing weed from the basin brings the fish into reach.
Eels coil in the muddy remains of an old moat. The bank is slick, and the water is deeper than it looks.
Several dressed blocks have fallen clear of the old wall. Moving them without cracking edges takes more planning than force.
Green copper nails remain in rotted roof beams scattered across the floor. Pulling them cleanly preserves enough metal to matter.
A dry chamber holds sacks of old lime sealed in waxed cloth. Some have spoiled, but enough remains useful if sorted carefully.
A collapsed roof has left several beams dry under slate. They can be salvaged before the next rain soaks them through.
The outer gate has split from its hinges but its core remains sound. Pulling usable planks free means dealing with rusted bands first.
Weathered scaffold poles lie stacked under a broken stair. The straightest ones are buried beneath rubble and old rope.
Ash-streaked dead stand in the doorways of a collapsed hall, facing outward like guards. They move only when someone crosses the threshold.
A tiny clay-winged thing darts through the cracked rafters, carrying a needle and a stolen signet ring. It defends its master's abandoned notes viciously.
A rusted suit of armor kneels before an empty reliquary. When the lid is touched, the helm lifts and the old oath wakes.
Floating-city sigils cover a tilted floor in repeating patterns. Stepping on the right sequence keeps the whole chamber from sinking.
A cracked arch gives way with a sigh of dust and old mortar. A quick dive turns a crushing fall into a bruising scramble.
A fresh black candle burns on a windowsill thick with years of dust. No footprints lead to it, and its smoke bends toward the cellar.
A ruined manor's cellar stair has been hidden under planks and new mortar. Someone went to trouble to bury the way down, not destroy it.
A signet pressed into old wax names a lord who never existed. Matching the seal to a nearby tomb could expose a fraud.
A ghostly watchman repeats the same warning from a broken tower each dusk. The warning changes if shown a recovered patrol badge.
A shrine niche holds three empty handprints and one fresh smear of blood. Finding what fits the handprints opens the saint's hidden store.
A flaking battle mural hides a map scratched into the plaster beneath. It marks a ruin within the ruin that should not fit.
A fig tree has rooted in a cracked courtyard and dropped fruit among fallen tiles. The ripe figs are safe if brushed clean of lime dust.
An abandoned shrine garden still grows rosemary and rue in orderly beds. Cutting respectfully avoids disturbing old offerings.
Mushrooms fill a damp cellar where wine racks collapsed long ago. The edible caps grow apart from the pale poisonous ones.
Tiny fish flicker in a rain-fed cistern below the ruin. The water is clear until one careless step sends dust drifting down.
Eels coil in the dark basin of a cracked fountain fed by an underground spring. They bite at torchlight and shiny hooks.
Old moat carp nose through weeds around fallen stones. They are fat, slow, and difficult to pull through the reeds.
A fallen marble cornice lies split but clean beside the hall. Useful blocks can be cut from the unweathered underside.
Old lead strips remain in shattered window frames. Twisting them free without losing shards takes steady hands.
Colored glass tiles from a floor mosaic lie under dust and rubble. The brightest pieces are still usable for inlay.
A collapsed library shelf has dry oak boards under the rubble. Freeing them whole means shifting stones in the right order.
The ruin's gate beams are cracked at the hinges but solid in the middle. Cutting them free wakes nesting birds and falling dust.
Benches in a ruined theater have weathered gray but stayed straight under the roof. Good planks hide under broken plaster.
